text: The surgical team uses a supraumbilical optical port with three robotic arms and two assist ports on the patient's left. The camera is oriented inferiorly towards the patient's feet, showing the left iliac artery in red, the left iliac vein in blue, and a bowel segment in orange hanging in the pelvis. The bowel is swept cephalad, away from the pouch of Douglas.

The bladder is visible, with the mobile dome being grasped and stretched. The Foley catheter balloon is deflated. The surgeon prepares to make the first incision using lab marks as a guide, incising the peritoneal reflection at the level of the vas. An inset displays the same maneuver performed using the da Vinci robot.

The surgeon traces the vas deferens proximally to the seminal vesicle, freeing it from surrounding tissue. The seminal vesicles and prostate base are separated from the Denonvilliers' fascia. Attention is then directed to the lateral sides of the prostate, where sharp dissection and minimal electrocautery are used to carefully separate the prostatic tissue from its neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon performs dissection on the contralateral side, proceeding caudally to reach the prostatic apex while preserving the neurovascular bundles on either side of the prostate. Meticulous lateral dissection is carried out into the prostatic apex, then attention shifts proximally to the junction of the bladder and prostate. The bladder is dissected at its distal extent beyond the trigone, carefully avoiding the ureteral orifices by following its funneling into the prostatic urethra.

The surgeon gently teases the bladder fibers, visible as longitudinal and crisscrossing bundles, off the smooth prostatic capsule to ensure adequate margins. The bladder is entered at its narrowest portion by incising the posterior lip of the prostatic urethra, revealing the Foley catheter within the bladder lumen. The demarcation between the prostate posteriorly and the bladder attached anteriorly is clearly visible.

The surgeon detaches the prostate from its proximal attachment to the bladder neck, leaving it connected only to the distal urethra. The fibers at the urethroprostatic junction are carefully teased away, revealing the elongated urethra, which is then sharply cut to preserve viable mucosa. The prostate is rolled off the prostatic fossa, completely freed, and placed into a specimen bag. The lap sac is inserted through the twelve millimeter assist port, sealed, and moved towards the left paracolic gutter.

The surgeon begins the vesicular urethral anastomosis using the Yonsei technique, employing a barbed suture with two needled ends. Continuous stitches are used to complete the anterior anastomosis, ensuring precise mucosa-to-mucosa approximation between the bladder and urethra.

The surgeon threads the Foley catheter into the bladder lumen and inflates the balloon after completing the posterior stitches. Using the Yonsei technique, the surgeon performs the anastomosis by stitching from inside to outside on both edges to prevent catheter damage, tying the thread ends at the six o'clock position. Hemostasis is achieved, and the peritoneal reflection is closed with a single layer of barbed suture. A JP drain is placed in the dogless pouch. The specimen bag string is positioned into view, and the camera is transferred to the assist port to pull the string out of the optical port for specimen retrieval at the umbilicus.
